title: Chapter 47: Yes
content: Chloe types the email with her nose.

It takes forty-seven minutes. Her snout hits the wrong keys constantly — the 'p' is basically a landmine, and don't even get her started on 'z' — but she's gotten good at this over the months, pecking out messages one careful tap at a time, and the slowness is almost the point. Every letter is a choice. Every word is deliberate.
*Lena —*
*I'll do it. Under my terms.*

*1. I review every test before it happens. Nothing without my approval.*
*2. I stay in touch with my apartment daily. Video calls, no restrictions.*
*3. I come home every other weekend. Non-negotiable.*
*4. The data belongs to both of us. Meridian can publish. So can I.*
*5. I am not a subject. I am a partner. That word goes in the contract.*
*— Chloe*

She reads it four times. Adds the em dash before her name — her signature, her style, the one thing that feels like hers even when her fingers are hooves and her hands are paws and her body is a negotiation between two species that can't decide who's winning.
She hits send.
Lena's reply arrives in eleven minutes. Chloe knows because she watches the clock, her capybara eyes tracing the digital numbers on Dave's laptop screen with the intensity of someone defusing a bomb.
*Chloe —*
*Done. Every term. I'll have the contract overnighted. We start the 15th.*
*I meant what I said. You're not a specimen. You're the most important medical case of the decade, and you're a person who deserves to make choices about your own life.*
*I'm glad you said yes.*

Chloe stares at the screen. The email is warm. Professional. Reasonable. Every term accepted without negotiation, which should feel like a victory but sits in her stomach like a marble — smooth and hard and wrong.

People don't agree to everything you ask. That's not how negotiations work. There should have been pushback on term four, at minimum. Data ownership is the whole value proposition. Meridian isn't a charity.
But Lena said yes. Enthusiastically. Like the terms were obvious, like of course Chloe would keep her own data, like of course she'd come home, like of course she's a partner and not a subject.
Chloe shifts back to human and rereads the email standing up, like a different posture might reveal what's bothering her.
It doesn't.
Dave is on the couch when she comes out of his room, scrolling his phone with the glazed focus of someone who's been in a TikkiTok spiral for an hour. He looks up.
"So?"
"I said yes."

Dave nods slowly. Doesn't congratulate her. Doesn't frown either. Just nods, the way he nods when she tells him she's eaten or she's taken a walk — acknowledgment without judgment, presence without pressure. It's the most Dave response possible, and right now it's exactly what she needs, and it also makes her want to shake him.
"You don't have anything to say?"

He thinks about it. Actually thinks, scrolling thumb pausing, which is how she knows it's real. "I think you're making a decision that makes sense on paper. I think you're the only one who gets to decide if it makes sense in your body." He looks at her. "I'm not going to celebrate and I'm not going to talk you out of it. You already talked yourself into it. That's enough for me."
It's supportive in the exact way that leaves nothing to push against. Chloe wants to push against something.
She calls Maya.
"You're the data, not the scientist."
Maya doesn't even say hello first. She picks up and the words are there, like she's been holding them in her mouth since Chloe texted her two hours ago.
"Maya—"

"Think about it. They're going to study your blood, your brain, your cells. What you eat. When you sleep. How you shift. They get papers and patents and funding. You get—what? Rent money? The satisfaction of being useful?"
"They agreed to every term I set."

"Because the terms don't matter!" Maya's voice cracks on the last word, and Chloe hears Amara in it — the same urgent practicality, the same I-know-better-than-you-coded insistence. "They can agree to anything on paper. Once you're there, once you're in their facility, you think they're going to stop at the terms? You think a contract is going to protect you from someone who sees you as a breakthrough?"
"I'm protecting myself. That's what the terms are for."
"Chloe." Maya's voice drops. "You're the data. You're never the scientist. Not in their building. Not with their equipment. Not on their clock."

Chloe doesn't have an answer for that, so she says, "I know," which isn't an answer, and Maya knows it, and they both sit in the phone silence for a while, and then Maya says, "I love you," which is what Maya says instead of I'm scared, and Chloe says, "I know," again, which is what Chloe says instead of I'm scared too.
She texts Patel.
*Decision made. Meridian. Contract signed next week.*
The typing indicator appears. Disappears. Appears. Disappears.
No response.
She texts again: *Patel?*

Nothing. He read it. She can see the read receipt. He read it and he didn't respond and that's so unlike him — Patel who responds to everything, Patel who has an opinion about everything, Patel who once sent her a fourteen-message thread about the correct way to load a dishwasher — that the silence is louder than any argument.
Mrs. Hendricks knocks at 7 PM. She's carrying a casserole dish — tuna, obviously, the woman has exactly one recipe and she deploys it like a weapon of emotional mass destruction.
"Heard the news," she says, setting the dish on the counter. "Maya called me."
Of course she did.

Chloe stands in the kitchen, shifting her weight from foot to foot, waiting for the verdict. Mrs. Hendricks will have something to say. She always has something to say. She'll say it's dangerous or brave or foolish or necessary, and Chloe will argue, and they'll land somewhere in the familiar territory of disagreement-respect-love that defines every conversation they've had since Chloe moved in.
Mrs. Hendricks doesn't say anything.

She walks over. She's shorter than Chloe by four inches but right now she takes up the entire room. She puts both hands on either side of Chloe's face — gently, so gently, her palms warm and dry and soft the way only old-lady hands get, soft from decades of things held and released.
She holds Chloe's face for a long time.
Doesn't speak. Doesn't need to. The hands say everything: I see you. I'm not going to stop you. I'm going to be here when you come back. I'm afraid. I trust you. I don't want to. I trust you anyway.

Chloe's eyes burn. She doesn't cry. Mrs. Hendricks doesn't cry. They stand in the kitchen and the casserole cools on the counter and neither of them says anything, and it's the most honest conversation they've ever had.
One more night.

Chloe sleeps on the couch. She could sleep in her own bed but the couch is where the apartment lives — the center of the wheel where all the spokes meet, where Dave watches his phone and Maya paints and Mrs. Hendricks dozes and Patel argues and the whole little household breathes in and out like a single organism.

She curls up under the crocheted blanket. The couch smells like everyone — Dave's detergent, Maya's turpentine, Mrs. Hendricks's lavender hand cream, the specific warm-papery scent of a space that has been lived in by the same people long enough to become a climate.
She should hum. She always hums before sleep. The hum is how she finds the capybara, how she lets the animal settle in alongside the human, how she closes the gap and rests.
She doesn't hum.

Too anxious. Too wound up. The hum requires a kind of surrender — a letting-go that she can't access tonight, her brain running scenarios and counter-scenarios, imagining the facility, the contract, the cameras, the blood draws, the weekends home, the data, the debt, the solution, the choice that is a good choice, the right choice, the smart choice.
*This is the right choice,* she thinks, lying on the couch in the dark, staring at the ceiling she can barely see. *This solves the debt. This is what a smart person does.*
The apartment is quiet. Somewhere down the hall, Mrs. Hendricks is snoring — the low, rhythmic sound that Chloe has heard every night for months, the sound that means home, the sound that means safe.
*This is what a smart person does.*
The hum doesn't come.
*A smart person.*
The phrase circles back, and she feels it then — a cold thread pulling through her chest, thin as fishing line.
*Not a capybara. A smart person.*

There it is. In the dark, in the silence, in the space where the hum should be: the quiet, lethal clarity that she is making this decision as a human, for human reasons, with human logic, and the capybara — the part of her that knows things without thinking, that rests without asking permission, that hums because humming is what her body does when it's at peace — the capybara wasn't consulted.
The capybara isn't consulted.
The capybara is the data.

She closes her eyes. Forces her breathing to slow. Tells herself this is just nerves, just the first-night jitters, just the adjustment period. Tomorrow she'll sign the contract. Tomorrow she'll start the next phase. Tomorrow everything will be in motion and she won't have to think about whether the motion is right or just inevitable.
In the dark, the hum doesn't come.
She sleeps without it, and the sleep is human and thin and dreams of rooms with windows that don't open.
